name: Sant'Eustachio Il Caffè
description: Order al banco. Pay first, then bring the receipt to the bar. The gran caffè is sweetened by default — say 'amaro' if you don't want sugar. The line moves fast.

name: Tazza d'Oro
description: Twenty meters from the Pantheon. The granita di caffè con panna in summer is the move. In winter just stand at the bar with an espresso and watch tourists try to figure out the queue.

name: Roscioli Caffè
description: Same family as Salumeria Roscioli. Cornetto and a cappuccino at the bar before 10am, or a properly made negroni after 6. Quiet enough to take a call.

name: Salumeria Roscioli
description: The cacio e pepe argument starts and ends here. Book two weeks out, sit at the counter if you can, order the burrata. Wine list is unreasonably good.

name: Forno Campo de' Fiori
description: Pizza bianca by the etto. Get it warm, fold it, eat it walking. The pizza rossa is a separate religion. Cash is faster than card.

name: Pizzeria Da Baffetto
description: Roman thin-crust pizza done at 400°C in 90 seconds. There will be a line. They will rush you. That's the experience. Order the bruschetta first.

name: Pantheon
description: Free entry to the best-preserved building of antiquity. Go at 9am or after 6pm to avoid the crush. Look up — the oculus is the only light source.

name: Trevi Fountain
description: Visit at 6:30am if you want a photo without humans. Coin toss is right hand over left shoulder, with your back to the fountain. The water is recycled.

name: Galleria Borghese
description: Bernini's 'Apollo and Daphne' alone is worth the trip. Mandatory two-hour timed entry — book online a week ahead. The garden is free and underrated.

name: Villa Borghese Gardens
description: Largest park in central Rome. Rent a rowboat on the lake, or just sit under a stone pine. The Pincio terrace at sunset is the right move.

name: Trapizzino
description: Triangular pizza-bianca pocket stuffed with stew. The pollo alla cacciatora is the canonical order. Eat it standing on the street with a Menabrea.

name: Da Enzo al 29
description: Twelve tables. Cash only. Carbonara made with guanciale from a place they refuse to name. Lunch starts at noon — be there at 11:50.

name: Bar San Calisto
description: €2.50 birra and Roman regulars who have been arguing at the same outside table since 1986. Open until 1:30am. Do not order a cocktail.

name: Suppli Roma
description: A hole in the wall that does one thing — supplì, the rice-and-mozzarella croquette — and does it better than anyone in the rione. €2 each.
address: Via di San Francesco a Ripa 137 00153 Roma

name: Antica Caciara Trasteverina
description: Hundred-year-old cheese shop. The owner will cut you slivers of pecorino romano DOP and explain why the supermarket version is a crime.
address: Via di San Francesco a Ripa 140 00153 Roma

name: La Bottega del Caffè
description: On Piazza Madonna dei Monti, the social center of the rione. Sit outside, order a caffè shakerato, watch the locals talk for two hours.

name: Trattoria Monti
description: Marche cuisine in the heart of Rome. The tortello al rosso d'uovo — a single giant raviolo with a runny yolk — is on every food critic's top-ten list.

name: Ai Tre Scalini
description: Tiny wine bar that overflows onto the street most nights. Polpette, salumi board, a glass of Cesanese. The waitstaff will judge your wine choice — accept it.

name: Mercato Monti
description: Weekend vintage and design market. Saturdays and Sundays 10am-8pm. Independent designers, vinyl, mid-century lamps. Cash gets better prices.

name: Fafiuché
description: Piedmont-focused wine list. Order the vitello tonnato, then a glass of something Nebbiolo-adjacent the owner picks for you. Closed Sundays.
address: Via della Madonna dei Monti 28 00184 Roma

name: Mercato Testaccio
description: Modern enclosed market that replaced the old open-air one. The Roman matriarchs do their shopping here at 9am. By 1pm it's lunch counters.

name: Mordi e Vai
description: Inside Mercato Testaccio. The allesso di scottona — slow-cooked beef on a roll dipped in its own broth — is the platonic Roman lunch. €5.

name: Flavio al Velavevodetto
description: Built into the side of Monte Testaccio — the ancient Roman amphora dump. Order the tonnarelli cacio e pepe and pretend you don't notice the pottery in the walls.

name: Da Felice a Testaccio
description: Roman classics, no shortcuts. The cacio e pepe is finished tableside. Reservations mandatory; the dining room is small and the regulars are protective.

name: Pizzeria Da Remo
description: Roman thin-crust at full Roman volume. Order on the tear-off paper menu with a pencil. Bruschetta arrives in 90 seconds, pizza in five minutes.
address: Piazza di Santa Maria Liberatrice 44 00153 Roma

name: Roseto Comunale
description: Public rose garden on the Aventine. Free, open April through June and again in autumn. Best at golden hour — the Circus Maximus is right below.

name: Aventine Keyhole
description: Look through the keyhole of the Knights of Malta priory door — St. Peter's dome is framed perfectly down a hedge tunnel. Free, ten seconds, worth it.
address: Piazza dei Cavalieri di Malta 4 00153 Roma

name: Necci dal 1924
description: Pasolini's old hangout. Now a full-day place — coffee in the morning, aperitivo at 7, late dinner. The garden in back is the secret.

name: Co.So Cocktails & Social
description: Massimo D'Addezio's bar. The Carbonara Sour — vodka infused with bacon — sounds like a joke and isn't. Tiny, packed, worth the elbows.

name: Pigneto Quarantuno
description: Neighborhood-priced cooking from a chef who used to be at Glass Hostaria. The pasta-of-the-day is always the right call.

name: Pizzarium Bonci
description: Gabriele Bonci's standing-room-only counter. Pizza by weight, sliced with scissors, eaten on the street. The mortadella with pistachio cream is the canonical first slice.

name: Gelateria dei Gracchi
description: Pistachio is sourced from Bronte; nocciola from Piedmont. No artificial colors — if it's grey it's because real pistachio is grey. Cash preferred.

name: 200 Gradi
description: Panini shop with a 50-item menu. Order the n.34 'Pulled Pork all'Italiana' or whatever the staff recommends when you look indecisive.

name: Vatican Museums
description: Book the 8am early-entry slot online. Skip the Sistine queue, see the Raphael Rooms while they're empty. Allow three hours minimum, bring water.

name: Castel Sant'Angelo
description: Hadrian's mausoleum, then a papal fortress, then a prison, now a museum. The rooftop terrace has the best free-standing view of St. Peter's.

name: Mercato Esquilino
description: Rome's most international market — Bangladeshi spice stalls next to Roman butchers next to Chinese greengrocers. Closed Sundays.

name: Panella
description: Bakery and salumeria with a wine list. The pizza bianca is laminated like a croissant. Sit-down breakfast with a view of Santa Maria Maggiore.

name: Pommidoro
description: Pasolini's other regular spot. Family-run since 1890. The bucatini all'amatriciana is non-negotiable. Cash only.

name: Said dal 1923
description: Working chocolate factory in a 1920s industrial building. Sit at a table inside the production hall, drink hot chocolate the texture of pudding.

name: Tram Tram
description: Pugliese cooking on the tram tracks. The orecchiette with cime di rapa and the seafood antipasti come out together. Loud, fun, family-run.

name: Eataly Roma
description: Four floors of Italian food retail next to Termini-Ostiense. Useful for a one-stop edible-souvenir run; less interesting than a real market.

name: Centrale Montemartini
description: Capitoline Museum overflow installed inside a decommissioned 1912 power station. Marble emperors against turbines. Almost always empty.

name: Doppiozeroo
description: All-day place. Aperitivo buffet 6:30-9pm is the legitimate Ostiense move — €10 includes a drink and unlimited carbs.

name: Porto Fluviale
description: Industrial-conversion gastropub. Roman classics plus pizza plus brunch. The aperitivo platter is enough to skip dinner.

name: Lievito Roma
description: Garbatella neighborhood pizzeria. Dough fermented 48 hours, ingredients from small producers. The locals have not yet given it up.

name: INDUSTRIE FLUVIALI
description: The venue for FarCon Rome (May 4–5). Multi-level post-industrial complex on the Tiber — rooftop terraces, open halls, and flexible event spaces. Metro B Piramide is a 5 min walk. Roma Ostiense station is also walkable. Bus lines 23, 77, 83, 96 stop nearby.

name: Caffè Greco
description: One of the oldest cafés in Rome (est. 1760), on the Via Condotti shopping strip. Keats and Goethe were regulars. Order at the bar — the marble interior is the point.

name: Gran Caffè La Caffettiera
description: On Piazza di Pietra with Roman temple columns as your backdrop. Good for a mid-morning espresso between the Pantheon and Campo de' Fiori. Sit outside if you can.

name: Panificio Bonci
description: The bakery side of Gabriele Bonci's operation — Roman-style pastries, exceptional pizza bianca, and a queue of locals before 8am. Different from Pizzarium; this is the breakfast stop.

name: Armando al Pantheon
description: Family-run since 1961, steps from the Pantheon. The cacio e pepe is textbook. Reservations are essential — they fill every service and don't pretend otherwise.

name: Trattoria Pennestri
description: Modern Roman cooking in Ostiense — close to the venue. The pasta changes weekly, the natural wine list is serious, and the room is loud in the best way. Book ahead.

name: Trapizzino Testaccio
description: The original Testaccio outpost of the trapizzino empire. Triangular pizza-bianca pockets stuffed with Roman stews — get the pollo alla cacciatora and eat it standing on the street.

name: Antico Forno Roscioli
description: The bakery arm of the Roscioli family, baking in-house since the 1970s. Pizza bianca, cornetti, and focaccia from 7am. Get there early — the good stuff goes by 10.

name: Il Grottino a Testaccio
description: Neighborhood pizzeria on Via Marmorata near the Tiber. Roman thin-crust, no frills, entirely reliable. A solid option before or after a night at Industrie Fluviali.

name: La Gatta Mangiona
description: One of Rome's best Neapolitan-style pies, in a slightly inconvenient but worth-it Monteverde location. Long wine list, relaxed room, no rush. Worth the extra tram stop.

name: Salotto 42
description: Aperitivo bar opposite the columns of the Temple of Hadrian. Order a Negroni, take a seat facing the ruins. Book ahead for a window table — it sells out most evenings.

name: Enoteca Ferrara
description: Wine bar and restaurant on Piazza Trilussa. Good for a proper sit-down dinner with a serious Italian wine list. The room in back is quieter if you want to actually talk.

name: Freni e Frizioni
description: Trastevere aperitivo institution. The spritz is €7 and comes with a full antipasti table. Gets very crowded after 8pm — show up at 6:30 if you want a seat and the good spread.

name: Litro
description: Natural wine bar in Monteverde, the calmer neighborhood above Trastevere. Rotating small-producer bottles, knowledgeable staff, good snacks. The back terrace is the spot.
